stem: At a lock entrance, what does a RED light mean?
choices: Entry forbidden, you must wait | Entry authorised | Exit from the lock only
explanation: A single red light means “no entry”; wait. A green light authorises entry; two red lights mean the lock is out of service.
source_ref: RGP, art. A.4241-53-31 §1 — feux d'écluse (rouge = accès interdit)

stem: Which light authorises entry into a lock?
choices: The green light | The red light | Two red lights
explanation: A green light authorises entry. Two red lights mean the lock is out of service.
source_ref: RGP, art. A.4241-53-31 §1 — feu vert d'écluse (accès autorisé)

stem: In a lock chamber, how should you handle your lines during locking?
choices: Tend and adjust them, never made fast to a fixed point | Make them fast hard to a fixed cleat | Remove all the lines
explanation: The water level changes: tend the lines and pay out or take in as needed, never make them fast to a fixed point (risk of parting or listing).
source_ref: RGP, art. A.4241-53-30/-31 — conduite dans le sas d'écluse

stem: At a lock, who sets the order and manner in which vessels enter?
choices: The lock-keeper and the lock's signs | The fastest vessel | Each driver, freely
explanation: The lock-keeper (or the lock's signs / automatic system); follow their instructions and the lights.
source_ref: RGP, art. A.4241-53-31 §4 et §12 — instructions de l'éclusier

stem: At a lock, what do TWO red lights mean?
choices: The lock is out of service | Entry is immediately authorised | Exit is compulsory
explanation: The lock is out of service / closed to navigation. A single red light means “wait”, and a green light authorises entry.
source_ref: RGP, art. A.4241-53-31 §1(a) — deux feux rouges (écluse hors service)

stem: What should the boater do with waste water (black water) on inland waters?
choices: Hold it in a tank and empty it ashore | Discharge it discreetly at night | Discharge it far from the banks
explanation: Keep it in a holding tank aboard, then empty it at shore collection facilities; discharging it into the waterway is prohibited.
source_ref: Code des transports, art. R.4241-63 / CDNI (décret 2010-197) — eaux usées (cuve, dépôt à terre)

stem: On inland waters, discharging hydrocarbons, waste water or rubbish into the waterway is:
choices: Prohibited | Allowed in small amounts | Free outside harbours
explanation: Prohibited: such discharges pollute the waterway and must be collected and disposed of in the proper facilities.
source_ref: RGP (Code des transports, art. R.4241-62) — rejet d'hydrocarbures interdit

stem: To limit bank erosion and disturbance to wildlife, the boater should above all:
choices: Reduce speed and wash near the banks | Run at full speed in the centre | Moor in the reed beds
explanation: Reduce speed and wash when approaching banks, reed beds and sensitive natural areas.
source_ref: RGP, art. A.4241-53-21 — réduire vitesse et batillage près des berges

stem: When refuelling, what precaution prevents water pollution?
choices: Avoid any overflow (do not fill to the brim) | Fill as fast as possible | Rinse the tank with canal water
explanation: Refuel unhurriedly and do not fill to the brim (use a funnel, watch the level) to avoid any fuel spilling into the water.
source_ref: RGP (Code des transports, art. R.4241-62) — prévention de la pollution

stem: Driving a boat under the influence of alcohol is:
choices: Prohibited | Allowed on canals | Tolerated at night
explanation: Prohibited: the skipper must be fit to steer and react, as when driving a vehicle.
source_ref: Code des transports, art. L.4274-14 — conduite en état alcoolique (≥ 0,5 g/L)

stem: Which document, specific to the boat, must be available aboard on inland waters?
choices: The boat's circulation card | A road map of the area | Only the engine manual
explanation: The boat's circulation card (navigation title), together with, where applicable, the driver's licence and proof of insurance.
source_ref: Code des transports, art. L.4221-1 — titre de navigation (carte de circulation)

stem: What licence is needed to drive a motor pleasure boat over 4.5 kW on rivers and canals?
choices: The permis plaisance inland-waters option | No licence is needed | The côtière option always suffices
explanation: The permis plaisance “eaux intérieures” option (the côtière option is for the sea).
source_ref: Décret n° 2007-1167 du 2 août 2007, art. 2 — option eaux intérieures

stem: To use a VHF radio aboard, the skipper must, as a rule, hold:
choices: The restricted radiotelephone certificate (CRR) | The boat licence alone | No particular certificate
explanation: The restricted radiotelephone certificate (CRR), which authorises the use of a VHF. Since 2011 it is no longer required in French territorial waters for a hand-held VHF without DSC; it remains required for a fixed or DSC-equipped VHF and for navigating abroad.
source_ref: UIT, Règlement des radiocommunications, art. 47 — certificat d'opérateur (CRR, délivré par l'ANFR)

stem: Besides the general police regulation (RGP), a particular waterway may also be governed by:
choices: A particular police regulation (RPP) | The road traffic code alone | No other rule
explanation: A particular police regulation (RPP) adapts the general rules to the local circumstances of a given waterway or reach.
source_ref: Code des transports, art. R.4241-31/-32 — règlement particulier de police (RPP)

stem: On inland waters, with respect to large vessels, ferries and vessels on a regular service, small craft (pleasure boats) must:
choices: Keep out of their way | Hold their course, having priority | Force them to stop
explanation: Small craft must leave the way clear for large vessels, ferries and convoys: they keep out of their way.
source_ref: RGP, art. A.4241-53-3 — menues embarcations s'écartent des grands bateaux et bacs

stem: As a general rule on an inland waterway, to which side should a vessel keep?
choices: To its right (starboard) | To its left (port) | In the middle of the channel
explanation: Each vessel keeps to its right (starboard) as a rule; vessels then pass port to port, unless signs indicate otherwise.
source_ref: RGP, art. A.4241-53-6 §1 — venir sur tribord (croisement bâbord-bâbord)

stem: In a narrow passage (under a bridge, a tight channel) where crossing is impossible, what must the driver do?
choices: Make sure the passage is clear before entering | Always enter first | Speed up to force through
explanation: Make sure the passage is clear before entering and give way per the signs; on rivers priority goes to the down-bound vessel (going with the current).
source_ref: RGP, art. A.4241-53-8 — passage étroit (priorité à l'avalant)

stem: On a flowing river, when an up-bound and a down-bound vessel meet, which has priority?
choices: The down-bound vessel | The up-bound vessel | The larger of the two vessels
explanation: The down-bound vessel (going with the current) has priority because it manoeuvres less easily; the up-bound vessel leaves it the way and the chosen side.
source_ref: RGP, art. A.4241-53-6 §2 — priorité de l'avalant

stem: On still water, two power-driven vessels meet head-on. What do they do?
choices: Each alters to starboard | Each alters to port | They both stop
explanation: Each alters to starboard (its right) so they pass port to port.
source_ref: RGP, art. A.4241-53-6 §1 — rencontre (chacun sur tribord)

stem: On inland waters, on which side does a vessel normally overtake another?
choices: On the port (left) side of the overtaken vessel | Always on the starboard side | By passing very close to it
explanation: As a rule on the port (left) side of the vessel being overtaken, once sure the manoeuvre is safe; the overtaking vessel keeps clear of the one overtaken.
source_ref: RGP, art. A.4241-53-11 §1 — dépassement par bâbord

stem: Aboard a small craft on inland waters, what must each person have?
choices: A personal flotation device | A compulsory drysuit | Nothing, if they can swim
explanation: Every person aboard must have a suitable personal flotation device (lifejacket / buoyancy aid).
source_ref: Arrêté du 10 février 2016 (Division 245), art. 5 — EIF par personne (eaux intérieures)

stem: Among the useful equipment aboard a small craft, you must have a means of:
choices: Bailing the boat out (scoop or pump) | Measuring the channel depth | Recharging the VHF
explanation: Bailing the boat out (scoop, pump or bucket) if water comes in, in addition to the personal flotation devices.
source_ref: Arrêté du 10 février 2016 (Division 245), art. 5 — dispositif d'assèchement

stem: To be able to stop safely away from a quay, a craft must have:
choices: An anchor with its rode | A second engine | A liferaft
explanation: A suitable means of anchoring (an anchor with its line) to hold the boat.
source_ref: Arrêté du 10 février 2016 (Division 245), art. 6 — ligne de mouillage

stem: A vessel's identification mark (registration) must be:
choices: Displayed visibly and legibly on the hull | Kept only in the boat's papers | Optional for pleasure craft
explanation: It must be displayed visibly and legibly on the hull, so the vessel can be identified.
source_ref: RGP, art. A.4241-47-1 — marque d'identification du bateau

stem: Before getting under way, what must the skipper check (duty of care)?
choices: The boat's condition, the safety gear and the weather | Only the fuel level | Nothing — checks are the lock's job
explanation: Ensure the boat and engine are sound, the safety equipment is aboard, and the conditions (water level, weather) are suitable.
source_ref: RGP, art. R.4241-9/-10 — devoir général de prudence du conducteur

stem: On an inland waterway, what does ONE prolonged blast from a vessel generally mean?
choices: “Attention” | “I am turning around” | “I am stopped”
explanation: One prolonged blast is the “Attention” signal; it draws other vessels' attention.
source_ref: CEVNI — signal général « Attention » (un son prolongé)

stem: On a waterway, what does a rectangular sign with a red border and a white horizontal bar mean?
choices: No passage / no entry | Obligation to stop and refuel | Recommendation to slow down
explanation: It is a prohibition sign (type A.1): no entry / passage forbidden.
source_ref: RGP, annexe 5, panneau A.1 — interdiction de passer

stem: At night, a power-driven vessel underway on inland waters shows a light to port. What colour is it?
choices: Red | Green | Yellow
explanation: As at sea: port sidelight red, starboard green, plus a white masthead light and a sternlight.
source_ref: RGP, art. A.4241-48-12 — feux des menues embarcations (bâbord rouge)

stem: At night, a vessel moored away from the bank, outside a harbour, most often shows:
choices: An all-round white light | A flashing red light | No light
explanation: An ordinary white light visible all round, to signal its presence to other vessels.
source_ref: RGP, art. A.4241-48-20 — signalisation en stationnement (feu blanc)

stem: On inland waters, how do you recognise a PROHIBITION sign?
choices: By its red border | By being entirely blue | By a green triangular shape
explanation: Prohibition signs have a red border (often a white bar with red), like the “no entry” sign.
source_ref: RGP, annexe 5, section A — signaux d'interdiction (bord rouge)

stem: What does a rectangular BLUE sign with a white symbol generally give?
choices: Information / guidance | An absolute prohibition | An immediate danger
explanation: Blue rectangular signs give information (mooring allowed, ferry, service point, etc.).
source_ref: RGP, annexe 5, section E — signaux d'indication (bleus)

stem: A square BLUE sign bearing a white letter “P” means:
choices: Mooring permitted | Mooring prohibited | Marina 1 km ahead
explanation: Mooring (berthing) is permitted at this spot.
source_ref: RGP, annexe 5, panneau E.5 — stationnement autorisé (P)

stem: A red-bordered sign bearing a white arrow indicates:
choices: An obligation to follow the shown direction | A mere recommendation | A bathing area
explanation: It is a mandatory sign: you must follow the direction (the side) shown by the arrow.
source_ref: RGP, annexe 5, panneau B.1 — obligation (suivre la flèche)

stem: A sign bearing a number (e.g. “3.50 m”) generally indicates:
choices: A limit on headroom, width or depth | The distance to the next lock | The waterway's number
explanation: A restriction: for example the available headroom, width or depth (series C signs). A maximum speed is instead a mandatory sign (B.6).
source_ref: RGP, annexe 5, section C — restriction (hauteur, largeur ou profondeur)

stem: Under the inland navigation police regulation (RGP), what is a “small craft” (menue embarcation)?
choices: A vessel under 20 metres | Any vessel under 24 metres | A vessel under 15 metres only
explanation: As a rule, a vessel with a hull length under 20 metres (excluding passenger vessels, tugs, etc.).
source_ref: RGP (Code des transports, art. R.4000-2, 7°) — menue embarcation (< 20 m)

stem: Where is mooring (stopping) of a vessel generally prohibited on a waterway?
choices: Under bridges and in narrow passages | Along every fitted quay | Nowhere — mooring is unrestricted
explanation: Mooring is forbidden where it hampers navigation (under bridges, in narrow passages) and wherever a sign prohibits it.
source_ref: RGP, art. A.4241-54-2 — stationnement interdit (ponts, passages étroits)

stem: To limit wash (the wake that erodes banks and rocks moored boats), what must the driver do near the banks?
choices: Reduce speed | Increase speed to pass quickly | Hug the bank as close as possible
explanation: Reduce speed when approaching banks, harbours and moored boats, to limit the wash.
source_ref: RGP, art. A.4241-53-21 — vitesse et batillage

stem: On inland waters, what is a “down-bound” (avalant) vessel?
choices: A vessel going downstream | A vessel going upstream | A vessel stopped against the bank
explanation: A down-bound vessel goes with the current (downstream); an up-bound (montant) one goes against it. On rivers the down-bound vessel, less manoeuvrable, has priority.
source_ref: RGP, art. A.4241-53-1 et -53-6 §2 — montant/avalant (priorité à l'avalant)

stem: Along a river or canal, how is your position located?
choices: By kilometre points (bank markers) | By latitude and longitude alone | By nautical miles
explanation: By kilometre points (PK), shown by markers or plates on the banks, counting the kilometres from the start of the waterway.
source_ref: Voie navigable — repérage par points kilométriques (PK)

stem: Before passing under a bridge or a power line, what must the driver check?
choices: That the air draft is less than the available headroom | That its speed is at maximum | That its anchor is down
explanation: That the air draft (the boat's height above the water) is less than the available headroom, shown by the signs.
source_ref: RGP, annexe 5, panneau C.2 — hauteur libre limitée (tirant d'air)
